title: Training
text: This section is in development.

title: Working with the RAP Development team
text: Our goal in NRS is for RAP to be the default approach to analysis. To achieve this goal, we will:

title: Working with the RAP Development team
text:
Defining our RAP approach
To succeed in our goal, it is important we all have a shared understanding of what RAP is and what we mean by a ‘RAP approach’. We will do this by:

Running awareness sessions for all statisticians and managers
Sharing clear guidance, both technical and non-technical
Developing tools for use across teams to promote consistency

title: Working with the RAP Development team
text:
Project support
Different teams and projects will require different levels of involvement from the RAP Development team. Support might look like this:

A RAP developer is embedded in a team to lead the planning and development of a RAP for a specific project. They train the statistics team to apply RAP principles and co-develop code to meet agreed standards.
RAP development is led by the statistics team with guidance from the RAP Development team through regular meetings and code review.
The statistics team engages with the RAP community, follows NRS guidance and contacts RAP Development team for ad-hoc support and advice when required.

Our level of involvement and timing of when support is offered will vary from team to team depending on the following factors:








Factor
Light Support
Embedded Support




R & RAP skills
Strong skills
New to R


Time available for development
Adequate time
Limited time


Overall SAS workload
Small
Large


Previous support from RAP Development team
Already supported
No prior support



We will arrange a session with each statistics team to discuss these factors, their current preparedness for RAP, plans for development, and support needs. By collecting this information from each team, we can begin to make a plan for the order we will work with teams, and what level of involvement will be most suitable.

title: Working with the RAP Development team
text:
Planned projects
The following projects have been identified as priorities for the RAP Development team:

NHSCR Monthly Extract: As there are currently no statisticians working in the NHSCR team, the RAP Development team have adopted their monthly extract SAS process as our first project. This will give us, as a team, the chance to develop our understanding of NRS systems and software, record our learnings, and once complete, present this as a case study of RAP best practice for other teams to learn from.
Vital Events project TBD: The Vital Events team have a large number of resource-intensive SAS processes. Therefore, we plan to engage with this team early to identify a project that will help develop skills and free up time for further development work.

title: Blog
text: Building Process Maps slides for RAP Show & Tidy presentation on 13th May 2026.

title: Process Mapping
text: This document introduces process mapping and explains why it is a useful tool for documenting and improving analytical processes. Process maps provide a clear, visual overview of how data flows from inputs through processing and analysis to final outputs. They support transparency, shared understanding, and more efficient ways of working. They can also support work within existing SAS projects, help teams track and document their analytical pipeline, and provide a clear foundation when transitioning to open‑source tools.

title: Process Mapping
text:
What is a process map?
A process map is a method for documenting and visualising the steps required to move from the beginning to the end of a process. In the context of NRS statistics, a process may relate to the production of publications, management information, data tables, or any other analytical work that is carried out on a recurring basis.
Process maps can vary in their level of detail; however, it is generally helpful to include the following key elements:

Input data: These may come from a database, a flat file, or be manually defined within code.
Manual steps: These can include tasks such as copying and pasting data between files, renaming or moving files, or carrying out manual quality assurance checks.
Code or programs: These may consist of SAS programs, R scripts, or other automated processing tools. In some cases, it may also be useful to include details of the main tasks the code carries out.
Outputs: These can include data files, charts saved as image files, formatted Excel spreadsheets, publication reports, or dashboards.

title: Process Mapping
text:
Why create a process map?
A process map is an invaluable piece of documentation for any analytical process. It can help you to:

Develop a shared understanding within your team of what the process involves. This is particularly useful for managers or others who are not closely involved in the day-to-day technical delivery.
Explain the process to others, such as a new team member or a colleague from the RAP Development team who may be supporting redevelopment work.
Spot opportunities to improve the process, such as areas where steps could be simplified, reordered, or combined.
Make it easier to develop the process in a non‑linear way, for example by showing where improving outputs or visualisations first could be more useful than starting at the beginning.

Even if a process has already undergone redevelopment, creating a process map remains valuable, particularly for building shared understanding and supporting knowledge transfer. In addition, further opportunities for improvement may still be uncovered.

title: Process Mapping
section: Top tips for creating a process map
text:
Top tips for creating a process map

Start simple. Begin with a high-level view of the process. If it becomes clear that additional detail is needed, this can always be added later.
Don’t worry about using specialist software. Simple text boxes and arrows in PowerPoint or Word are often sufficient. Tools like Microsoft Visio can also work well, but they are not essential.
Use symbols consistently. Use different symbols to distinguish between data, code, and manual steps.
Have a clear key or legend to explain what each symbol represents. The specific symbols you choose are less important than using them consistently.
Keep the scope manageable. Avoid trying to capture multiple processes, or an overly large process, in a single map. A good rule of thumb is to create one map per publication or equivalent analytical product. If a map becomes too large or complex, consider splitting it into separate maps, for example, one for data processing and another for producing outputs.
Keep your process map with your code where possible. It makes it easier to find later. This also follows RAP principles by keeping documentation alongside the code base, rather than in a separate location. If it is not possible to store the process map with the code, it should be kept alongside the rest of the project documentation.
You don’t need to understand every line of your code to produce a useful process map. It is about capturing the flow, not the detail.

title: Process Mapping
text:
Example process map
The example process map below is based on NHSCR, which is a SAS process run on monthly and quarterly basis. It reads in multiple datasets, applies a series of transformations, and produces a set of output data files. This example focuses on illustrating a section of that flow.

This process map uses different symbols to represent input files, data manipulation, intermediate files, and outputs. Using consistent symbols keeps the map clear and easy to follow, and helps readers quickly understand the flow of data through the process.
Looking at the map also helps illustrate some of the practical benefits of process mapping in action. For example:

It allows someone new to the process, or unfamiliar with the code, to quickly see which data files are required and what outputs are produced.
It highlights where similar steps are repeated across multiple process flows, which can prompt discussion about whether parts of the process could be simplified or reused to reduce repetition.

title: Process Mapping
text:
Using Microsoft Visio
Microsoft Visio is a useful tool for creating process maps within NRS, as it is available through Microsoft 365. When you open Visio, you can choose from a range of templates, including a basic diagram or a standard flowchart template with commonly used symbols.
When creating process maps, the priority should be to get started rather than focusing on unnecessary complexity. Using simple, clear shapes and flows with keys will help ensure that your process map is easy to follow.
Visio files can be saved in visio file format (.vsdx), which allows you to revisit and edit them later. You can also export them as images (.png) or PDF files. However, exported versions cannot be edited, so make sure to keep the original Visio file for any future updates.
For more guidance on using Visio, please refer to these slides on building process maps.

title: NRS RAP Book
text:
This book contains information on developing Reproducible Analytical Pipelines (RAPs) in National Records of Scotland (NRS).
The content has been developed by the RAP Development team, who joined NRS in 2026. The team’s role is to lead the implementation of RAP across statistics and analysis production in NRS.
Please get in touch with the RAP Development team if you have any feedback or questions.

title: NRS RAP Book
text:
What is a process map?
A process map is a method for documenting and visualising the steps required to move from the beginning to the end of a process.
They can vary in their level of detail; however, it is helpful to include the following key elements:

Input Data
Manual steps
Code or prompts
Outputs

title: NRS RAP Book
text: A further example

title: NRS RAP Book
text:
Where to find Visio
Through the SCOTS Microsoft365 (or M365) account we have access to the online version of Visio, Microsoft’s flowchart building software.
It can easily be located via the NRS Saltire home page by clicking the 9 dots at the top left hand side of the screen. From there you can use the search bar and type Visio or you can click More Apps scrolling to the bottom of the page to find the Visio icon.
Starting a new template document will give you a standard palette of shapes to get starting in building your process map.

title: NRS RAP Book
text:
Building a process map

Process maps can use existing code or be used to plan projects from the outset.

First identify fixed constants

Ask what changes are required to convert input into desired output. Add each major change to the map as an element. You can build on the detail, using different shapes, colours or expanding the map.

title: Why RAP?
text:
Reproducible Analytical Pipelines (RAPs) are analytical processes that have been developed in a way that promotes reproducibility, efficiency and quality.
Some common examples of a RAP approach in practice are:

Using open-source software (e.g. R or Python) over proprietary software (e.g. SAS)
Automating tasks where possible to minimise manual steps
Using version control software (e.g. Git) to create an audit trail of changes
Good documentation stored in the same place as the code
Regular peer review of code to improve quality and shared understanding
Built in quality assurance to give early indication of problems

These are just some examples, however there are many more that could be applied depending on the requirements of the project.
It is important to note that RAP is not all or nothing, or once and done. You do not need to apply all possible RAP principles to achieve benefits, and improvements should be an ongoing process. Adopting a RAP approach will involve some quick wins, some up-front investment for bigger long-term gains, and continuous improvement.
You can read more about RAP and examples of good practice on the Government Analysis Function website.
Embedding a RAP approach in NRS will help us to:

Transition from SAS to Open Source software
Improve efficiency of processes to free up time for other work
Ensure processes are well understood, easy to maintain and robust to staff changes

Teams may already be following some RAP principles in existing SAS processes. However, transitioning from SAS to open-source software will offer further opportunities to strengthen this approach and realise more benefits.

title: Getting started
text:
While the RAP Development team won’t be able to work closely with all teams right away, there are steps teams should take to get started on RAP development.

Develop process maps for each analytical output. These will be useful to help understand existing SAS processes, highlight areas for improvement and plan RAP development work. We have written guidance and produced an example map to help you get started.
Consider R training needs in your team.

Contact the NRS Learning & Development team to find out about upcoming training courses on offer in NRS.
Review the Scottish Government R training on offer and keep an eye on the OCS newsletter for other opportunities.

Consider whether there are any technical barriers to your team using the current R environment on NRScotland (e.g. data access, memory constraints) and raise these with the RAP Development team.
Think about other planned or potential changes to your analysis and how this might impact RAP development. For example, consider proposed changes of the publication review work.
Follow coding good practices when writing R code. We will develop more thorough guidance for NRS soon, but this is a good place to start.
Attend RAP Show and Tidy sessions to learn from others and consider presenting your own development work (no matter how big or small!)
Familiarise yourself with existing RAP resources:

Government Analysis Function RAP Guidance
RAP Self Assessment Tool
Quality Assurance of Code for Analysis and Research (Duck Book)
